questions.question: Where are local variables of a method stored during its execution?
questions.explanation: Each method call creates a stack frame that holds its locals and parameters.

questions.question: What does a Java reference variable actually store?
options: A) The object’s full data inline
B) A copy of the object’s fields
C) An address/pointer to a heap object
D) The class definition of the object
questions.explanation: References are pointers to heap objects; the object data lives on the heap.

questions.question: Given int[] a = {1,2}; int[] b = a; b[0] = 9;, what is a[0]?
questions.explanation: a and b alias the same array; modifying b modifies the same heap array.

questions.question: Java parameter passing is best described as:
options: B) Pass-by-value for primitives, pass-by-reference for objects
questions.explanation: Java copies the value; for objects, the value copied is the reference.

questions.question: Consider void foo(Point p){ p = new Point(5,5); }. If Point q = new Point(0,0); foo(q); what is q.x after the call?
questions.explanation: Reassigning p only changes the local copy of the reference; q still points to (0,0).

questions.question: Which comparison checks whether two String variables have the same sequence of characters?
questions.explanation: equals compares content; == checks reference identity for objects.

questions.question: Which statement about Strings is true?
options: A) Strings are mutable; concat changes the original
B) Strings are immutable; concat returns a new String
C) Strings live on the stack
D) Strings cannot be passed to methods
questions.explanation: Strings are immutable; operations produce new objects on the heap.

questions.question: When does a heap object become eligible for garbage collection?
options: B) When it goes out of scope
C) When no reachable reference points to it
D) When the program ends a method
questions.explanation: Eligibility occurs when the object is unreachable from any live reference chain.

questions.question: What is the primary risk of shallow copying a structure containing mutable objects?
options: C) Unintended aliasing and shared mutation
questions.explanation: Shallow copies reuse references, so changes via one reference appear in the other.

questions.question: Given int[] a = {1,2,3}; int[] b = Arrays.copyOf(a, a.length); b[1] = 99;, what is a[1]?
questions.explanation: Arrays.copyOf creates a new array; no aliasing, so a is unchanged.
